title: Needle-felted swan
subtitle: A seated white swan with an arched neck in white and orange
excerpt: Build a seated swan approximately 12 cm tall using white merino tops for the body and wings, with an orange and black beak and black eye marking. Construction works through a compact body core, a separate curved neck, and surface detailing. A forgiving beginner animal project that uses mainly one colour.
sourceNotes: Synthesised from needle-felted bird and waterfowl construction methods taught in UK guild and workshop settings. The compact-core / separate-neck approach is standard for larger-bodied birds. No armature method follows published beginner guides for standalone felted animals. No single public domain source.

glossaryTerms.definition: Fine-stapled wool from Merino sheep. Felts readily and produces a smooth, dense surface.
Combed wool fibres aligned in parallel. Smoother and finer than roving; good for surface detail and colour work.
The tiny notch cut into a felting needle shaft. Barbs catch wool fibres and push them together with each stabbing stroke.
Coarser, undyed wool used to build the inner bulk of a needle-felted piece before finer surface fibres are added.

content.text: A seated swan is one of the cleanest beginner animal projects in needle felting: a large white body with a simple curved neck, minimal colour work, and no armature required. At approximately 12 cm tall the finished piece is substantial enough to hold detail without needing the very fine control of smaller work. The construction divides into three stages: the body core, the neck, and the colour details on the beak and eye. You will build the neck separately and attach it once both pieces are firm, which gives you full control over the arch before committing.
Felting needles are extremely sharp. Always use a finger guard and work with slow, controlled strokes over the foam pad.
Method
Step 1: Build the body core. Take a generous handful of white
core wool

and roll it into a teardrop shape roughly 9 cm long and 6 cm at its widest point. The broad end will become the swan's breast and the narrower end the tail. Place the roll on your foam pad and work across the surface with a 38-gauge needle, rotating the piece as you go, until it holds its shape without springing back. The body does not need to be perfectly smooth at this stage; you are looking for structural firmness.
Step 2: Cover the body in white merino. Pull thin wisps of white
merino

and lay them over the core, smoothing with your fingertip before needling them in. Work from breast to tail, overlapping each wisp by about half. Use the 38-gauge needle to felt the layers firmly, then switch to the 42-gauge for a final smoothing pass. The surface should be dense and even with no loose fibres pulling away.

Step 3: Build the neck separately. Roll a short rope of white core wool approximately 8 cm long and 1.5 cm across. Needle it lightly into a firm but pliable sausage shape; it should hold a gentle curve without cracking. Cover it with white merino wisps and smooth with the 42-gauge needle. Shape a slight S-curve by hand while the piece is still workable.

Step 4: Attach the neck to the body. Position the base of the neck at the centre front of the body, where the breast meets the top. Hold it in place and use the 38-gauge needle to work in short diagonal strokes through the neck base and into the body, building a firm join on all sides. Then lay a few thin wisps of white merino over the join and needle them flat to disguise the seam.

Step 5: Form the head. At the top of the neck, press and needle a slightly larger oval to suggest the head. It should be roughly 2 cm wide and 1.5 cm tall. Work slowly with the 38-gauge needle until the head reads as a distinct rounded lump rather than a continuation of the neck.
Step 6: Add the beak. Take a small pinch of orange
wool tops

and roll it into a short triangle approximately 1.5 cm long. Needle it to the front of the head with horizontal strokes, shaping the tip to a slight point. Take a tiny pinch of black wool tops and needle a narrow strip along the top ridge of the beak to form the characteristic mute swan knob. Keep the black area small; a few mm of coverage is enough.

Step 7: Add the eye marking. Take the smallest pinch of black wool tops and needle it into a dot approximately 3-4 mm across on each side of the head, set just behind and above the beak. Work in tiny circular strokes with the 42-gauge needle until the dot is tight and round. The
barb
on the finer needle gives cleaner edges on small colour spots.

Step 8: Define the wings. The seated swan's wings fold flat against the body rather than standing proud. To suggest wing structure, use the 38-gauge needle to draw two shallow channels curving from the top of the back down and around each side, following the natural teardrop of the body. You are not removing material, just compressing a line into the surface. Needle these lines firmly until they read clearly.

Step 9: Final smoothing. Work over the entire piece with the 42-gauge needle, paying attention to any areas where the surface looks raised or uneven. Rotate the piece in your hand and check the silhouette from several angles before setting it down.
Troubleshooting

items.problem: The neck keeps leaning to one side after attachment.
items.solution: The join needs more material. Pull thin wisps of white merino around the base of the neck and needle them firmly into the body from all sides, not just the front. Three or four thin layers built up gradually give a stronger hold than one thick layer.

items.problem: The black eye dot has fuzzy edges and looks smeared.
items.solution: Switch to the 42-gauge needle and work in very tight, almost vertical strokes directly over the dot, going slightly into the white surface around the edge to compress any stray black fibres back under the dot boundary.

items.problem: The surface has small holes from the needle.
items.solution: This is usually caused by needling in the same spot repeatedly at the same angle. Work with a slight rocking motion and move the needle to adjacent spots after every few strokes. Smooth over with a fine wisp of white merino and the 42-gauge needle.

content.text: Finishing and care

The finished swan needs no sealing or coating. Set it on a flat surface and press the base lightly to flatten any rocking. If the piece will be displayed in a dusty room, a quick brush with a soft dry paintbrush keeps the white surface bright. Do not wash or wet needle-felted work unless it has been specifically designed for wet finishing.
